text.title: Tripwire anti-patterns that erase the cost win
text.summary: Three guardrail mistakes turn a 100x cost reducer into pure overhead: tool-using guardrails, output-guardrails on streaming, and same-model guardrails.

section.title: Three failure modes
section.p: The tripwire's value is `cheap-model-cost / strong-model-cost ≈ 0.01`. Each anti-pattern below either inflates the numerator or destroys the streaming UX, erasing the win.

rule.statement: Guardrail must be one constrained-output call to a small model — never give it tools, never run it on the same model as the main agent, and never wire output_guardrails on a streaming endpoint without explicit buffering.
rule.rationale: OpenAI documents that output guardrails see the FULL final output; streaming + output-guardrail forces buffering and breaks token-by-token UX. A tool-using guardrail amortizes nothing — it costs a multi-turn loop on every request including legitimate ones. Same-model guardrails defeat the entire pricing differential.

antipattern.description: Guardrail agent declared with `tools=[search, fetch, lookup_user]` so it can "investigate" before deciding tripwire.
antipattern.reason: Now every legitimate request runs a multi-turn tool loop on the cheap model. Average pre-agent cost rises 5-20x; the only request type that stays cheap is the trivially-rejected one, which was already cheap with a single classification call.

antipattern.description: Adding `output_guardrails` to a chat endpoint that streams tokens to the user without buffering.
antipattern.reason: Output guardrails see the final output, so the stream must be fully buffered before the guardrail runs and ANY of it can be sent. First-token latency goes from 200ms to multi-second, defeating the streaming UX. Open Agents SDK issue #495 documents this.

antipattern.description: Using the same model for guardrail and main agent ("just to be safe gpt-5 will catch the jailbreaks too").
antipattern.reason: The cost ratio collapses to 1.0 — the guardrail no longer saves anything; it doubles the cost of legitimate requests because they pay for both the guardrail call AND the main agent. Use a model at least 5-10x cheaper than the main agent.

example.code: # Streaming + output_guardrail buffers the entire response main = Agent(model="gpt-5", output_guardrails=[refund_check]) async for chunk in Runner.run_streamed(main, msg): yield chunk # blocked until output_guardrail finishes on the FULL output
example.note: The "streaming" loop yields nothing until generation finishes — UX is identical to non-streaming, but with hidden buffering and worse error handling.

section.reference: Agents SDK output guardrail vs streaming issue.
